agent_output: {"summary":{"verdict":"BLOCK","one_liner":"Memory leak from never-cleaned-up WebSocket listeners and CourierTracker instances will OOM the service under production courier churn"},"critical_findings":[{"title":"Memory leak: WebSocket listeners and CourierTracker instances never cleaned up","risk":"With couriers disconnecting/reconnecting every ~5 minutes, each reconnection creates a new CourierTracker with new listeners on the WebSocket. Old instances are never torn down — the `on('message')` and `on('error')` closures capture `this`, preventing GC of every previous CourierTracker. This is the direct cause of the 3-month memory climb.","severity":"Critical","location":"CourierTracker.setupListeners() — no corresponding teardown","reproduction":"1. Create 100 CourierTracker instances for the same courier (simulating reconnects). 2. Observe heap snapshots growing linearly. 3. After 1000 cycles, old closures and CourierTracker objects are still retained.","fix":"Add a destroy/cleanup method that calls `this.ws.removeAllListeners()` or removes specific listeners. Listen for the `close` event on the WebSocket to trigger cleanup automatically: `this.ws.on('close', () => this.cleanup())`. Ensure the caller disposes the old tracker before creating a new one.","time_to_fix":"30 minutes","acceptance_criteria_affected":"Service stability — memory usage must remain bounded under courier churn"},{"title":"Unhandled JSON.parse throws on malformed WebSocket messages","risk":"A single malformed message (non-JSON, binary frame, empty string) will throw an uncaught exception inside the `message` listener. Depending on the Node.js error handling setup, this either crashes the process or silently kills the connection handler.","severity":"High","location":"CourierTracker.setupListeners() — `JSON.parse(data)` with no try/catch","reproduction":"Send a non-JSON string (e.g., empty string or `'ping'`) over the WebSocket. JSON.parse throws, no catch exists.","fix":"Wrap in try/catch: `try { const location = JSON.parse(data); } catch (e) { console.warn('Invalid message from courier', this.courierId); return; }`. Validate that `lat` and `lng` are numbers before calling updateLocation.","time_to_fix":"10 minutes","acceptance_criteria_affected":"Service resilience — one bad message must not kill a courier session"},{"title":"Fire-and-forget database write loses errors and applies no backpressure","risk":"`db.couriers.updateLocation` is called without awaiting or catching. If the DB is slow or down: (1) errors are silently swallowed, (2) rapid location updates queue unbounded promises, creating memory pressure and potential DB connection pool exhaustion.","severity":"High","location":"CourierTracker.updateLocation()","reproduction":"Introduce 500ms DB latency. Send location updates every 100ms. Observe promise queue growing unboundedly and connection pool saturation.","fix":"Await the call (make updateLocation async), add error handling, and consider throttling/debouncing updates (e.g., max 1 write per second per courier). At minimum: `try { await db.couriers.updateLocation(...) } catch (e) { console.error(...) }`","time_to_fix":"20 minutes","acceptance_criteria_affected":"Data integrity and DB stability under load"}],"important_issues":[{"title":"No input validation on location coordinates","description":"lat/lng are used directly from parsed JSON with no validation. Values like `{lat: 999, lng: -999}`, `null`, or missing fields will write garbage to the database.","location":"CourierTracker.updateLocation()"},{"title":"No WebSocket close event handling","description":"The `close` event is never listened for. This means the service has no awareness of courier disconnections — no logging, no cleanup, no state transitions.","location":"CourierTracker.setupListeners()"}],"suggestions":["Add rate limiting per courier to prevent GPS spoofing or hyperactive clients from flooding the DB","Consider batching location updates (e.g., buffer for 1s then write latest) to reduce DB write volume","Add structured logging with courier ID correlation for observability","Implement a connection registry (Map<courierId, CourierTracker>) so reconnections explicitly replace and dispose previous instances"],"production_readiness":{"deployment_risks":"The memory leak is already in production and worsening. Fix should be deployed promptly but with care — ensure existing connections are handled during rolling restart.","rollback_capability":"Safe to roll back since fix is additive (adding cleanup). Rolling back just reverts to current leaking behavior.","backward_compatibility":"No external API changes. Fix is internal plumbing only.","monitoring_coverage":"Add heap size metrics and per-courier connection count tracking. Alert on connection count per courier > 1 (indicates leaked old instances).","breaking_changes":{"api_breaking":false,"db_destructive":false,"shared_library":false,"auth_security":false,"data_pipeline":false,"config_changes":false,"details":[]},"safe_to_deploy":false,"rollback_plan":"Revert commit and restart service. Memory leak resumes but is survivable short-term with periodic restarts."},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":95,"high_confidence_areas":["Memory leak diagnosis — classic Node.js listener leak pattern matching reported symptoms exactly","JSON.parse crash risk — deterministic failure on any non-JSON input","Fire-and-forget DB write risks"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact DB driver behavior under backpressure (depends on which ORM/driver is used)"],"assumptions":["db.couriers.updateLocation returns a Promise","New CourierTracker is created on each reconnect without disposing the previous one","No external connection manager handles cleanup outside this class"]}}
